So, this is welcome everyone to the bonus bonus call. Last week was a bonus call, but we didn't get through all the questions. So, we're having this week to get through the questions. And I got a bunch of questions you guys sent in front of me. And I'm going to start with that. Julie, I don't know that we can get to any more questions, but can people still send in questions if they want to? Sure. I can monitor if anybody has any last thoughts. They can send them to feedback at makesysotalpackage.com. And I can choose some of them to you as I always do. Sure. Let's give that email again. Oh, you just gave it. That's feedback at makesysotalpackage.com. Great. And so, let's just dive into it. The first question is from Hans. And he wants to know, would you talk more about the standard rate and data service you mentioned and how it should be used to choose and contact potential clients? Sure. Standard rate and data service publishes a series of guides to the various media. And they have that there's an SRDS for television and radio and Internet and all of this, you know, all the various media. And the direct mail SRDS is essentially a four-inch thick book that has somewhere in the neighborhood of, what is it, Bob? 60,000 mailing lists that are available for rental. And each one of those, well, many of the lists, some of the lists are compile lists, which means that someone just went to a database someplace. And compiled names of companies or individuals, like for example, the Dunn and Bradstreet lists, are compiled lists of companies. And you can get any size company you want, any number of employees, any amount of sales. Those are compile lists. They're also compile lists of doctors and lawyers and people by profession. Those tend to be their addresses at work. The most important lists for us are the lists of direct response companies who make their customer files available for rental. And you'll be able to look up those companies by the type of businesses they're in. If you want to look at newsletters who rent their files, you just simply go to that section and you'll see a listing of all of the newsletters that are out there along with the size of their files. They'll have quite often the number of hotline names, which is the number of names that new customers they brought in in the last 90 days. That'll tell you how fast they're growing. They'll tell you the mix, mail to female. SRDS is a really excellent source if you're writing copy too, because you can look at this and get some really good demographic and psychographic debt on the people you'd be talking to if your client is mailing to a given list. Are you there Bob? Yes. Okay. You can look at each one of those listings and see how large and successful the various companies are. It could be a catalog company, a newsletter, a health supplement company, any kind of company you want to write for. They'll have listings in there if they rent a list, and most companies do. So it's just a great place to go to get names of companies and also to research when you're writing copy. Hans, go ahead. I know that you're really expensive to buy, but most libraries have them. They have issues of the book going back years quite often at the larger libraries. This is what I did. If your library doesn't have it, you find a friend or a friend of a friend who's in an ad agency and they subscribe to it and they get a new directory every three months and they throw away the old one, has no value to them, they'll give it to you. They'll save you $400 or $500. Let me answer Hans's other question. We're not trying to sell you anything additional here, but your question also says in front of me, how do you use SRDS, but what other tools can you use to select clients and how do you contact those clients? I have written a book about this, but you don't mind me mentioning it, right? No, go ahead. It's called Secrets of a Freelance Writer. It's a $12 paperback and it tells you everything you want to know about selling yourself as a freelance copywriter that we may not have covered here, although we've covered, a lot of what we covered here is not in my book, but it'll tell you where you can find listening directories and what kind of, how you can contact them and it's available for you know, $11 or $12 on Amazon.com. So you might want to check that out. In standard rate and data service, if you want to check them out online, I believe it's SRDS.com. You can check there. I think we mentioned it once before too, but another great resource is target marketing online and you can just Google target marketing and find their website. They have a directory of major mailers. It's about an inch and a half, two inches thick and it comes with a companion CD-ROM. Just pop into your computer and you can search for the kinds of companies you're looking for. Yeah, and that actually I think is a terrific source for freelance as a prospect, I think. So you can take a look at that as well. And that is, it's not cheap, but it's not as expensive as standard rate and data service. It's meant to be affordable to people who want to market to the direct marketing industry. Here's a question. What is Clayton, what is the usual fee structure for multi-step mailings? A mailing campaign with a series of two to ten pieces to a niche target market. I think what she means is, you know, let's say you get hired by, like you mentioned, used to work for someone who made water purifiers. And let's say they had to list the people in their market and they wanted to send a series of six self mailers or letters or postcards. How do you charge for that? I would charge a percentage or revenues on something like that because you'll have the largest mailings in a series like that would tend to be a lead production, the lead production phase. And, you know, that's, that's a hard cost to the mailer. And so he's not going to want to add to his cost on that step by giving you a royalty. And I think if you're doing the entire series, the simplest way to structure it would be to say, okay, look, I'll do the series for you, but I want five, ten, five or ten percent of revenues. And you can define that anyway you want to. It would normally be net revenues, the total revenues minus the marketing and fulfillment cost. Also minus the product cost, right? Yeah, that would be the fulfillment cost. And, but I would structure it on a percentage of what I produce. Alternatively, you could do it on a percentage of the increase. So if right now, for example, he's, his six-step program is generating, you know, an ROI of, or let's not do ROI, if a six-step program is generating 50,000 a month, you come in and give him a kick-butt program and you generate 100,000 a month, you would take a larger percentage of the increase. If you were working just on a percentage of net revenues, it would be like 10% safe just to pick a number. If you're going in just to, if you go in and take a percentage of the increase, his risk is reduced substantially. Right. There's no way he can lose on that deal. And if he was, if he was doing 50,000, let's say 100,000 a month before and you come in for 10% of revenues and you don't lift his response rates, then he's out 10,000 bucks because he's got to pay you the 10,000, you know, the 10% of the 100,000. But if you come in and you say, well, I'll just take a percent of the increase, that is an easier deal for you to sell because there's no risk to him. And you can ask, in return for taking the risk yourself, you can ask for a higher percentage. Got it. That's a good answer. And note, it says here that was asked by no name, so no name, we hope you're satisfied. Find a name. That's a good thing for your business card. Here's an interesting question from our friend, a DD. He says, can Clayton say more about his procedures for using junior copywriters? For example, working arrangements, training, compensation, contractional arrangements, exclusivity, or non-compete causes? And this is being asked from the point of view of someone who Clayton, like you, is also a senior copywriter and may want to do this. Oh, good, good. Yeah, when I'm working with a beginning copywriter, somebody that may have a AWAI spec assignments but nothing else to show, I, well, I begin by trying to match up writers to assignments. I have coaching clients, health product clients, health newsletter clients, and investment clients. And so I look at the background of the person. The investment stuff, for example, tends to get very technical, and if they don't have a background there, it would be a mistake to pair them up with an investment client. On the health side, things are a little easier because a lot of what we write about is not technical, and the research kits we get are quite extensive. So most writers can, I could probably find a match there. The easiest of all, of course, is the coaching product because we're talking to people predominantly who are opportunity seekers and entrepreneurs trying to find a way to make money, and we can all relate to that. There's not a lot of technical stuff there. But I review their material, try to match them. The ones that impressed me, I then try to match with the client. I look for a project that's been assigned or about to be assigned, and I match the writer with that project. Then I'll have a preliminary telephone call with a writer in which I basically lay out what the job will be. Yesterday, for example, I retained a young lady named Lisa to help me on a package for healthy directions. And we had a telephone call lasted maybe 15, 20 minutes. I told her I was sending our federal express, and the client would also be sending one. And after a look at it over the weekend, and I told her that we would be talking on Monday. So on Monday, she's going to be, she and I will be talking, and she will receive a document from me that will be a very detailed layout of what I want the copy to look like. It'll say the main headline will be 48 point impact, regular, not bold. It can be the red, blue, or green, not black. The deck copy will be 14 point times New Roman bold. You will not allow Bill Gates to do your indentions or your bulleted lists or your numbered lists. You will do those manually. And I mean, it gets right down to the detail what I want it to look like. Body text will be 12 point times New Roman. Subheads will be 14 point impact, and they too can be red, blue, or green. The package will contain all collateral components, including the everything they get pays, the response device, front and back covers, carrier envelope copy, if applicable. Just basically, it's a standing document that I send out to all new writers, and it's important that they do things that way. So I don't have to spend a lot of time making the copy look presentable to the client. And also, it allows me to look at the page count and know exactly where we are in terms of how well this copy will fit into the format that we're going for. I've had experiences where copywriters will send me running text in 14 point to disguise the fact that they wrote way too short for the format we were writing. 14 point triple spaced. Yes. But David wants to also know, what about, do you have a written contract with these people, and what's the compensation? Yeah. Let me get there in just a second. The next thing that will happen with this writer is that I will include them in a conference called with client. I introduce them to the client. We will discuss the positioning of the product. We'll discuss the major themes that we want to go with. And in this case, it's a health newsletter. So the next, once everybody's generally on board with a theme, then we will have a conference call with the editor of the newsletter, the doctor who edits this newsletter. We'll, on that call, pick his brain. We know what we're going to write about. Now we want his solutions and his take on each of the things we're going to write about. After that call, while it's so fresh in our minds, a writer and I will get on the phone and I will literally dictate an outline. And I'll say, okay, here's the first, you know, I don't even address the headline.com. I just start with the opening running text. And I say, okay, here's the first thing we're going to say. Here's the second thing we're going to say. Here's the third point we're going to cover. The fourth point I take them all the way through until we get up to the point where we begin asking for the sale. Right. And then I ask the writer to please send me an outline of everything we just discussed. And this is a good kind of reality check to make sure that the writer is getting it. And then I take their outline. I make whatever changes I want to make to it. And then I send it back to them. And they write from that outline. And again, this isn't copy. It's just the first thing we want to say is we want to talk about how terrible it is to have heart disease. And the second thing we want to say is how terrible what the medical community does to people with heart disease. And then we want to talk about the fact that there's a better way. And then we want to introduce the editor and say, hi, my name is Dr. Joe Schwartz. And here are my qualifications. And then we want a credibility element. And that could be a series of testimonials or it could be a short story about a patient whose life he saved. And then we introduce this solution to that particular problem. And then we say, and I want to tell you more about this in your free copy of blank. And that's our premium. Our first premium we're going to give away. And then next we're going to move on to the next health problem that this doctor is going to address. And we're going to do the same thing. We're going to empathize. We're going to warn them about the solutions that are currently available to them. We're going to present a better solution. We're going to tease five or six more solutions and say that they're in the second premium they're going to get. And we're going to keep going like that until we've covered all of our premiums. And then we're going to introduce the product, justify our price, relieve the risk, ask for the sale. And so with that kind of outline, the rider now is equipped to go. And they at that point will also have the full research kit. As far as contracts and compensation are concerned, on the first assignment with a new rider, I typically will pay between $4,000 and $5,000 because I'm essentially paying them for the privilege of riding it myself. And I tell them, I say there's going to be more red on this page than on a slaughterhouse floor. You have to not let that discourage you. This first project, I have three strikes in your out policy, but the first project is never the first strike because that's a learning process between the two of us. And so, frankly, for warfare, they'd be paying me $100,000 to teach them what I'm going to teach them on this project. Instead, I'm willing to pay between $4,000 and $5,000. At the end of the project, then we do it and we drop a contract. There's no royalty for the first three or four projects at all for a young rider. The simple fact that the amount of time that I have to put into this to create a winner just doesn't justify it. Plus, it's like getting paid to go to Harvard. You're getting paid good money to do an internship with a top rider. So you don't give golf scholarships? No, but if the person's in the area, we will have a Toga party. But anyway, the contract is good until canceled, so it's not on a job-for-job basis. When I feel that the rider has progressed to the point where they're making me money, we're getting some controls together, I will move them up depending on what I hope will be a very fair assessment of what they're bringing to the table. And get them to the point where they're making half the advance in each package, which would be $12,500. And I pay when the copy is approved with young riders, not half and half, because, well, you know, it's sometimes with a younger rider, the process takes much, much longer. So, and then once they hit the $12,500 per package advance and they're still progressing, then I begin introducing royalties into the picture, up to 50% of the total royalty. So depending on whether it's a white market product where I'm getting a smaller royalty, typically $30 a thousand, or a narrower market product where I'm getting $50 a thousand or more, they'll receive up to 50% of that to keep them interested. We don't want to kid ourselves here. The young riders' objective in this relationship is to learn everything they can, get some samples, make a name, and then never write for me again because they're making so damn much money on their own, they wouldn't even consider writing for someone else. So the objective of this is like a mother eagle, you know, raising her offspring. The ultimate objective is to push them out of the nest. And so that's essentially my process. Now, I am working with senior riders who are very happy with $12,500 and between $15 and $25 a thousand on projects. I copy chief for them because they tend to have very high hit rates. And more of our packages become controls. So even senior riders who make $400,000 or $500,000 a year, up to $600,000 a year, come to me and ask me to copy chief them. And I think it's a good exercise for everybody involved, even if a rider is getting controls and making good money on the outside and fairly advanced, just the experience of working with someone else and learning a different approach strengthens you. And so I have four or five senior riders whose names you would know who have approached me over the last few months and asked me to copy chief them on projects. And so when I do that, it's a 50-50 split. So this is open to copy riders at a wide range of levels? Yes. I mean, I have, I think the best, the hottest rider of promotions for financial packages right now. I'm going to be copy chiefing in June of next year. I'm working with another one who's very well known to everybody on this call that I'm copy chiefing right now in a health package. So yeah, it's very helpful for everyone. That wouldn't be me, would it? Yeah. I have no secrets. Oh, okay. I'm proud. I mean, people, to know how seriously we take this, because I think it's important and people are saying, well, you know, why would you do this? Yeah, I'll make the money Clayton makes, but I make a lot of money. I mean, I'll make over $700,000 this year. But when you work with any other rider who's good, you're going to learn a lot. And when you work with someone of Clayton's caliber, you're going to learn even more. And I think all of the very successful copy riders who are operating today, you correct me if you think I'm wrong, have been copy chief or mentored at some time or another by other people at or above their level. Yeah, I mean, Gary Ben-Savango was mentored by Dan Rosenthal. There you go. That's how that's what got him started. So, you know, after he left David Ogilvy's organization. So yeah, I think it's very helpful. And especially if you've been doing it for a while, it's a great way to kind of get a fresh take on everything. It totally freshens you. Yeah. If you do it for a while and you have some level of success, you start to repeat yourself. Right. And theoretically, you should study other copy riders' work, but studying their work is not the same as being mentored or copy chief by them. So, I think the key is the mental processes involved. You know, you can study their copy, but that's good. But you don't really know how they arrived at particular decisions or ideas as they were developing that copy. And so working with them allows you to see those mental processes make some transparent to you. Now, we have a question from Peter. That's questioning, slightly cynical, but I'm going to read it. If you are making the very, very big bucks, you are Clayton. And believe me, folks, he is. Why are you sharing all your secrets for us with us so cheap? Gary Ben-Savango sold his for $5,000. What's the real reason behind holding all these seminars? I get that a lot. Wait, I got to write down my list. Kill Peter. No, we're only joking. Peter, we're only joking. No, I get that a lot. Actually, it's Carleen's fault. She came to me about a year and a half ago. And actually, we were at the Willard in Washington, D.C., Wendy and I were, and just hanging out. And Carleen came over and we had a nice day together. And she had just been to Gary's conference. No, she was planning to go to Gary's conference. That was it. And she said, you know, because Gary has never really mentored writers. And she said, you know, the guy who should be doing this kind of stuff is you. Because she felt that I had the ability not to do more than, you know, just share what I do, but to actually teach others to do it. And so she kept nagging me and nagging me and nagging me until I finally said, all right already, I'll do it. I could do it easy. But anyone who reads the total package knows that up until the AWI conference, I was not somebody who wanted to ever be in front of an audience or do public speaking. And so the idea of doing a conference was not something that I really felt was something I wanted to do. So the easing was a nice way for me to be able to start connecting with other writers and sharing what I knew. I was also having the problem and still am with clients who want me to do far more than I can do. And although, you know, seven figures is very nice, you know, there's eight figures out there available to me if I only had the capacity to produce more coffee. And I had learned when I was with Weiss that the coffee cups I had worked with previously, Paris Lampropoulos and, you know, Bob Hutchinson and Kent Comet and Carline and others made me money because when I was, you know, book solid, which was 100% of the time, and another project came in, I could accept it because I had somebody that could work with me on it. And so I thought that I could do well by doing good here, by helping young writers come along, and at the same time, out of this group of thousands of young writers, find people who I'd want to be in business with and who would increase my capacity. And that's precisely what's happened. I have seven coffee cups. Right now, all of them have come out of the files of the total package. All of them have completed at least the first AWAI course. And like I was telling the people at AWAI, I love that course. I recommend it to everyone. However, it's like going to college, you know, and I think I've used this metaphor before, but it's like going to college and getting your degree in medicine. They don't allow you to just go out and start cutting on people. You have to go serve an internship. And this is the same thing. I mean, we make more money than the best paid cardiologists in the country to write coffee. Right? They go to college for 12 years and serve an internship in a residency where they are allowed to practice their craft. Yeah, they really don't start making money. Everybody says doctors are so well paid. First of all, they don't start making money until they're in their 30s. Yeah. Well, we're in a business where you can take Master's and courses. Right. And then be in an internship with a top copywriter in a matter of months with the potential to make far more than any doctor or cardiologist in the country. And so, I mean, it's a wonderful pursuit, but to think that, you know, taking the course is going to get you immediately, you know, the minute they say, okay, you're done, immediately, you know, under the ranks of mid to high six-figure writers is just a little bit over-optimistic. And the internship is a transition that I can provide for people. And it makes me money, and it makes them money. My clients benefit because, you know, as I've said so many times, the scarce resource in the director-sponsed industry is not product ideas. It's not editors of newsletter. You know, they're newsletters. They're growing on trees. You can throw a rock and hit somebody who wants to edit their own newsletter. The scarce resource is a copywriter that can sell it to prospects. So, it's just good for everybody. That's how I got into it. Yeah, and I think the really apt for people listening, especially AWAI recent graduates, is this idea of a residency. You know, you don't go for medical school. You know, doctors train, and this is training. Now, terrific. Now, we have a question here from Mark. He says, aside from you, Clayton, do you know of other top copywriters who take on copycups? Paris Landpropolis does. Okay. Anybody else? Carlene Cole. Paris, Carlene Cole. Steve Wechler does. Okay. And he's got ads in the back of DM news every issue. I'm not aware of others who do, but I think, I'm trying to remember, somebody was telling me the other day that someone else is taking on cubs. I don't know who that would be. I don't want to say the wrong name. But the answer is yes. Other people are. And as word gets out, I mean, we're in a situation where every time I talk to a client now, I have one client who has just on a quarter million dollar a year retainer. And we've only been on the retainer for 30 days, and he doubled it yesterday. Because I had come along with a couple more writers that I could throw at his project, and we were on the phone, and he was complaining that he wasn't getting enough output from us. And so I said, well, we can do the work now because I have these two new web writers that we've been mentoring for the last two months, and they're really good, and we'll just bring them in under Danny Levis, who's my main web guy for this particular client. My only web guy for this client. And so I'm going to slip them in under Danny, and he'll be able to produce two to three times more work for this client. But just literally in a short conversation yesterday, the retainer went from a quarter million to a half million a year. So as most, you know, as other writers see what we're doing, I expect this practice to become more commonplace throughout the community. Got it. Here's a question. I thought we did this one, but I guess we didn't. How do you know beforehand when a concept or headline will be a winner? If you've come up with a number of headlines or concepts, for example, how do you decide which ones of those are worth testing? For me, it's this rule. It's just... I thought you'd say that. Yeah. It's not... I'm not a formula writer. I think rules are for unimaginative slugs. I hate rules. I'm a libertarian by nature. And if I don't feel it in my gut, it sucks. If I do feel it in my gut, it's good. And the stronger I feel it, the stronger it is. So, you know, I welcome every once in a while to a situation where I'll say, you know, I have three concepts, all of which grab me, but I don't know which one is going to resonate with my market best. And in that case, I beg and plead with a client to do a three-panel test on the first trial of the copy. It's not always possible, but they will usually do two if you ask real nice. But again, it's a visceral thing. It comes down to feeling... If you read that headline and you feel like you're at the site of a train wreck that's about to happen, you cannot look away, you know, then you have a winner. If you're rationalizing and intellectualizing it, overthinking it, and you end up with something that may be benefit-latent but flat, you know, you have all of the rational reasons why it's a good headline because you read all the books and it follows all the rules. But if it's not grabbing you by the short and curly, it's not a good headline, you know? So, I think that's how most would ultimately answer. It's visceral. You either have an instinct for what sells or you develop it over time. You know, that's... I was thinking about that this morning. I'm glad you said that because the bottom line is that we are salespeople. That's all we are. We do it with ink or with bits and bytes on the Internet, but we're salespeople. And that's it. I mean, the number one skill that you can acquire as a copywriter is to become a good salesman. If you can do that, then it doesn't matter how great a linguist you are, you know? You just talk to your prospects as a good salesman and you close them. You don't have to have wonderful pros. Your stuff doesn't have to even be grammatically correct. But if you can motivate them to make a purchase, then you're going to be a great copywriter. And frankly, the best copywriters are not the best writers. The best copywriters are the best salespeople. And when you talk to copywriters who have succeeded and who have done really well in their careers, many of them have one thing in common. At some point in their life, they were adored or salesmen. They sold used cars. They sold insurance. They sold something, you know, face to face. And they developed a feel for the prospect. So, you know, sharpening your sales skills to me is far more important than simply sharpening your writing skills. Good point. And I would wholly disagree. Here's a question, just switching, totally switching gears. Do you ever write copy directly in design software? I get asked this all the time, such as Quark or InDesign. It would seem like this would be especially useful for graphic and tenses pieces such as magilogs and tabloids to get a better idea of how it will eventually look when it's laid out and to keep you from over or underwriting. Yes, but I went through a phase where I wrote everything in Quark. And Quark is a pretty good program to do that in because it's so simple to use. It's not all of futzing around with text boxes and stuff and linking that you have to do with InDesign or PageMaker. I found myself paying too much attention to the design too early in the process and it was distracting to me. So, I stopped doing it that way. I know other writers who continue to write using design software. One of them in particular is an excellent writer. Others, you know, I'm not that impressed with their output. My feeling is that, first of all, writing to fit is a mistake. Don't ever even think about the length of the piece while you're writing. That's just, that's a distraction. It's not something you have to concern yourself about. When you're writing, the most important thing you can do is to get every benefit, every reason, why, and every reason and emotion out on paper. If that thing runs 120 pages, God bless you. It's always easier to cut than it is to add. Right, but conversely, you can use the same approach. Write it to what you think works and then, you know, if it's too short and insubstantial, there's probably more in the research you have that can go back to beef up points that were your week. To me, that's getting the cart before the horse. And again, this is subjective. You know, the answer to the question really is, do whatever works for you. But in my experience, you know, what's worked for me is focus on the prospect, connect the prospect to the product, milk every single benefit, dimensionalize every single benefit, connect every benefit with an emotion that the prospect has, be obsessed with the sale. Then when you're through, look down and say, oh, this is 34 pages long. I'm close. You know, I may have to cut a page, but I'm pretty good. Or look at it and say, holy cow, it's 64 pages long. I'm going to have to really do some serious cutting here. But what you're cutting from is a document that has every single sales point that you could possibly come up with. Right. And so you don't necessarily have to cut sales points. You just condense and you prioritize. When you write to fit, it's an artificial limitation. You know, it says the length of this thing is more important than it's selling power. And people learn that. It's so odd. You remember when you were in sixth grade and the teacher said, I need a paper on this or an essay on this, three pages. That was the only thing that was important to her, which was a very bad way to teach you. Yeah. And then you sat there writing, it was a very, very, very, very, very cold day. You know? Great expectations. It was an interesting book. It was very interesting to read it. And I was interested. Yeah. Again, the length is an artificial, absolutely artificial, play in that. I'm glad to hear you say that. Here's an interesting question from Ewan. He says, what do you do when you're researching? Now, he gives us for a financial newsletter, but this could work with any product. What do you do when your research takes up very compelling evidence against your client's editorials in the financial newsletter business? This guy, he says, so what if you're writing for an investment newsletter and your research finds that the guy is wrong? What he says? Well, you've got two choices, really. If you're going to be a successful copywriter, you need to put your own opinions aside and realize they don't matter. You're not the financial expert. You're not the person with the product your client is. You don't have his qualifications to make these determinations. He has an opinion, and your job is to sell that opinion. If you can't do that, whether out of good conscience or because you feel negative about his opinions, then you should resign the account and give his money back. But a professional copywriter is just that. He's a professional. He recognizes that what he's selling, when he accepts an assignment, is job. He becomes an actor. To me, a copywriter who says, I don't agree with my client's point of view, therefore, I can't write his copy, is like an actor saying, I can't play Jack the Ripper because I would never kill anybody. There was a funny radio commercial on where the actor asked to read some lines in the radio commercial, and he says, well, what's my motivation? And the producer says, we're paying you. Yeah, that's exactly right. You're a professional, and you're in an actor's role. You're putting yourself in the shoes of somebody who does hold this opinion. And you have to discipline yourself. Look, there are times when you look at something someone's saying, and you say, this guy is so obviously wrong, my conscience prohibits me from being involved with this product or presenting this opinion. Let's say, for example, you are a devout Republican, and the guy is a Democrat, and he's voicing moral positions or political positions that you find abhorrent. If that's the case, and you have a moral aversion to what he's saying, then don't accept it in the first place, or resign the account. If you find it out later, resign the account and give him this money back. Short of that, recognize that just like an attorney is an advocate for his client, you're an advocate for your client, and your job is not to have an opinion. Your job is to sell the editor's opinion or the opinion of the figurehead or the advocate's product. Got it, and I would agree. It's interesting. You know who Jerry Hunsinger is? Sure. He's one of the top fundraising cooperators of all time, and in a conference call I did with him, he told someone else this, and he said, the same thing you did about being a client's advocate. He said he never had as much fun as when he was writing for two clients at the same time. One was on the left side of the issue, whatever it was, pro-choice or anti-choice, whatever it was. The other was on the flip side. Again, you're not the expert. You want to advocate or interpret the client's point of view. Here's an interesting question. Let's say you are a new, we don't have to say young, we often use the term young, but you're just new to freelance copywriting. You're what you would call a young writer, even though you may be older, and you want to start off and a client approaches you and say, hey, I need a direct mail package to sell a health product or a newsletter subscription. Maybe let's say it's a letter package, and it's going to be an A12 or 16-page letter. If I'm new and I'm lucky enough to get asked to do that, what combination of upfront fee and royalty should I charge given my newbie status? I guess are we talking about our industry? Are we talking about newsletter industry here? We can talk about. He doesn't specify industry, but let's talk about, for example, let's just talk about you're asked to do a newsletter package. Maybe you're not asked by Phillips or Agora, but you're asked by one of the smaller newsletter publishers. They have a couple of newsletters. Let's say it's a mutual fund newsletter. They come to you, and what should you charge? Before you answer, I think a good way to sort of judge this in advance is to ask what copywriters they're using now. The names are Clayton Makepeace or Peter Betchell, or they're using A-list copywriters. You know something about that client's ability to pay a decent fee. I'm not saying you should charge the same fee, but that tells you something. That's right. If they're coming to you and you're a beginning writer and you have no track record. That's what the question says. Let's face it, most of the clients, the answer is kind of a two-part answer, I think. There are two kinds of clients out there. There are clients out there who are used to working with freelance copywriters. If they are, they have a certain number in their head when they talk to a writer. They know that they're going to have to pay, for example, a $25,000 advance and a royalty. If someone comes to them though, and they really like your copy, even though it may not be for their industry and you may have no track record, they're taking a huge risk by hiring you. And if they decide to hire you anyway, they must really believe in you. But remember, when you write the copy, it has to be designed. That's going to cost, if it's direct mail, between $6,000 and $12,000 to do the graphic design for the package. Then they've got print prep, which can be $10,000, $20,000. Then they've got to print up the pieces themselves. And if they do, say, a $50,000 name test, that's going to cost them probably another $30,000, $40,000 to mail it. So, by the time they're through, they're into this, not even counting your fee, $50,000, $60,000, that they're betting that you can write them a winner. So that's why they're willing to pay the high fee to writers with known track records. They want you to put time into it and take it seriously and make it worth your while, you understand? Well, I'm saying that track record is everything. These guys are willing to pay the big fees because I can give them four or five times the probability of getting a control. And four or five times the probability of not losing money, then a writer with a beginner. Yeah, with less of a track record and especially with no track record. So, short answer, I would think if they're willing to pay you $5,000 to $6,000, would be a good place to start on the advanced side. Right. And how about the royalty side? Full-blown. So, full-blown royalty but a lower advance, five or six grand. That's in line with what I've heard other people quote. So I think that's a very good answer to that. I have to tell you that don't ever accept the argument that you should accept a lower royalty. I think we covered that earlier in the series. Yes, we did. And people can go back and listen to that. But you explain that in detail and very eloquently. Just to interject, from a time perspective, because I don't know how long we're planning to go today. Colleen, what do you want to do? What do we say, one? Do you want to go a little longer? It's five to one now. I'm thinking you want to go to, I think we told people an hour, but you want to go to, can you go to 115? Oh, sure. Okay. So, Julie, we'll do that then. Does that work for everyone? Yes. Okay. So, let me get, next couple, and let me click on my email reader because questions are coming in even as we speak. Let's see what those are. And there's a couple. So, we answered what we should charge, five or six thousand dollars upfront in the same royalty that an experienced writer would get. Joanne asks, what's the best source of finding upcoming marketing conferences to network with potential clients? And then within that is the question, should people go to conferences and network, or is that a waste of time in your opinion? Conferences are an excellent opportunity for writers. And there are, you know, there's any number of DMA conferences around the country, the next, I think, National in San Francisco next fall. When is the DM days in New York? I don't know when it is, but if you go on the, just Google Direct Marketing Association, you can go on their website and find all their conferences. I mean, there are other organizations, but that's the big one. Yeah, that's the big one. Check them out and they'll give you a full listing of all of their conferences. Also, check out NEPA, that's the old newsletter on newsletters, it's the Newsletter Association. If you're interested in writing for newsletters, NEPA has conferences as well throughout the year. Right. And they're at www.newsletters.org. That's an easy one to remember. Yeah. And also, we're going to be doing our first ever, and who knows, maybe our last one, live event in April of 2006. We're looking at the 20th, 21st and 22nd. And we'll be announcing, we're going to be actually asking you to help us design the conference in a special email that you'll be receiving in the next couple of weeks. And asking your advice, asking you to tell us what it is you would like us to show you at that conference. And so, kind of pencil us in there because I would really love to have you all there. I think it's going to be a great time. Mark that date, tentatively April 26th, 27th around the end of the year. No, no, no. 2021, 22. 2021, 22. Yeah. And we'll be sending a questionnaire in a couple of weeks and asking you to help us with it. And then a week or so after that, we'll be tabulating the results and announcing the event. Julie, you want to take my credit card now? I want to go. Wait a minute. I don't want to give my credit card number over to this phone line. Everybody else is sitting there with a pencil. We all be going on this time. All right. I'll do that privately. Want to give me that number, Bob? No, I got smart. Let me say a couple of more things about conferences. The big DMA conferences are good, but they're huge. And you're going to be a small fish in a huge pond. And everybody is trying to sell somebody something at those conferences. The good news is they're not prohibitively expensive. And there are enough of them during the year that our local conferences, there's a direct marketing days in New York. There's a DM days LA. They're all over the country. Washington has a big active chapter, the DMAW. Yeah. And so they're not prohibitively expensive. They'll be fairly close. And you'll be able to meet some people. But just know when you go to that, you're going to be, every printer in the area is going to be there. Every list broker is going to be there. And they're all going to be chasing anything that sniffs like a client. And you're going to feel like the, you know, a 12-year-old girl in a lobby full of rock stars. I would have said chum in a shark pool, but yeah. And so to me, the better opportunity or conferences like the AWAI conference where you go, and there are serious copywriters there, and there are clients who also go to those. And the AWAI conference is still relatively new. I was very impressed this time at the job fair to see Motley Fool there, Weiss was there, and they're looking for copywriters. So I think those kinds of conferences would be excellent. I think my conference would be excellent too, because we'll have a good mix of major clients and copywriters there. And when you're at a conference like this, I think, you know, there are two ways to go with it. First of all, go prepared, go loaded for bear, have your samples, have a pitch letter for the people that are going to be there who hire writers. That demonstrates the quality of your writing as well as, you know, introduces yourself. And go there hoping to make a contact. In the Paris Lampropoulos interview, he talked about how he went to a Gary Halberk conference and came away with three assignments from that one conference, and he still has that one client now today, you know, that decade later, and that client pays him seven figures a year. But that wasn't a big DMA conference. That was a copywriter conference. The other thing that I would urge you to do when you go to conferences, and this can really only happen easily at a copywriter conference. By that I mean, you know, where there's a large percentage of people, there are other copywriters. We talked today about me mentoring other writers, even major writers like Bob. Every one of you is a copywriter or an aspiring copywriter, and you guys need to be talking to each other. You need to be creating cells of copywriters, friends that you meet at these conferences. Go out, have a cocktail with them, sit down, work out an arrangement where you can share your copy with each other and crit each other's copy and sharpen each other that way. I think that's a great suggestion. An outstanding opportunity that's just being missed, you know. And so, you know, when you go to these conferences, these sociable, these are not your competitors or your friends. They're in the same boat you are. They can sharpen you and you can sharpen them. And then 20 years from now, when you're all sitting around on your front porches talking about how much money you made, you know, you'll have friends to talk to about, you know, how you guys got started together. I agree with that terrific suggestion. Here's a question. This comes up a lot from people who get into copywriting. You mentioned how it's good to have a sales background in copywriting. But copywriters, as you know, come from varied backgrounds. Some come from just corporate work. Some come from sales backgrounds, but a lot of them come from writing backgrounds. They were newspaper reporters or they were journalists. And they decided to do another type of writing. And these guys always ask me. Here's a particular question from Michael. But I've been asked this many times. People will say, well, I'm a writer. Where can I learn about marketing? Because I don't know marketing. Where can I learn modern marketing strategies that can make both the client and me more successful? There are a couple of sources of that. First of all, Gitfab Stone's book, Successful Direct Marketing Methods. It's a wonderful book on how direct marketing works. Bob also has written several books that explain the nuts and bolts and the rationale behind direct marketing. You mean me or Bob Stone? You. Yes. And you can get those. I'll just mention. If you go to my website, www.blibesandboyly.com, there's a lot of content articles on there on the subject of marketing. You click on articles and those are free. You don't have to pay for them. And if you want to see the books I've written, you go to www.blibesandboyly.com and click on books and you can see and you can order them directly from the site. Now, I hesitate to mention this, but there are a couple of guys on the Internet, both who claim to be the greatest marketing experts in the history of the universe. I got to hear this. Go ahead and mention it. Let's have fun. Okay. And they have enormous egos and when they sell something to you, you're going to pay, believe me, top dollar for it. But they have free easings and those easings quite often contain a lot of great ideas that you could take to your clients to strengthen their campaigns. So, I think they're probably cheaper sources of the same information. Although, they even, they're free you're saying? Yeah, they're cheaper sources of the information that they sell. Right. But what they give away in their easings is very valuable. So, what are these easings? Okay. Go to www.abraham.com and sign up for Jay's free easing. And Google Dan Kennedy. Dan is a copywriter, but in, and I've only heard him speak once and he's great. He is great on the stump and he's hilarious and I totally agree with everything that I've heard him say. But Dan goes beyond just copy and in fact most of the things that I've heard him talk about had more to do with marketing. So, those are two more free sources that you can go to. Read successful direct marketing methods. That's the book that opened my eyes when I first got into this business and showed me exactly how the numbers work and what the limitations and opportunities are, how responses counted, and then all of those boring numbers that I had in my, my arithmetic issue last week. And it, it just gives you a good sound, they can, and as I pointed out before, most of the money I've made in this business, the really big paydays came from not just writing the copy but conceiving the campaigns and designing the marketing strategies behind them. So, getting a grasp on that stuff is an excellent idea. Great. And I'd also, I mean there's a set of books that are the standards of the industry and people quote them. Some guys have heard them before. The books by David Ogilvy especially, Ogilvy on Advertising, Tested Advertising Methods by John Capels, but Bob Stone is certainly at the top of that list. Stone is a marketing guy more than a copywriter. Right. A lot of the other books I've seen out there are more, you know, like Ogilvy and Capels and Reeves. Those are books on writing ads, you know, but Stone's book is really good on the nuts and bolts. So. Supposedly I've never read it but what's his name? Ed Bernett also has a compendium. I think it's Direct Mail and Psychopedia that's also of a similar nature that you might take a look at. He's a good guy. I just wanted to do a little thing. If you were an English teacher or if you were a journalist and you decided that you want to be a copywriter, one of the best things, and this is my advice, Bob, you may disagree but my advice is get a job at night on a car lot or selling insurance or doing something in the sales side of things. You don't have to make it a career. It doesn't have to be full-time, but at least have the experience of approaching a prospect and earning your yes and overcoming objections and doing the things that are necessary to make a sale. If you really don't want to do this in person and you're bashful, I think you can get a lot of the same experience. I did this, getting a job, and I didn't do it for a long time, telemarketing. Not from a script but telephone selling. I sold a high-end product where I wasn't reading a script, where I was giving guidelines, and you get what people really think and how they'll talk to you. Here's a question from someone who, John, who wants to break into writing for the newsletter industry, which you had a lot of experience in. He says, I have the Oxbridge Directory of Newsletters. How do I select which newsletter publishers to approach about doing copyright? Good question. I guess to a degree it depends on your experience level. Let's assume you're a beginner in the field. The majors are not going to want to hire somebody without a track record. The majors being Philip Zagora, Boardroom, Rodale, which isn't newsletters, but One Shot Books and subscriptions. You've got to leave out healthy directions. They're not going to want to hire out of the box somebody that isn't experienced yet or doesn't have really great samples to show. If you have tremendous spec assignments for AWAI, there is nothing wrong at all with sending them to Elliott at Healthy Directions, which some of you have done. Let me tell you what happens next. Ed sends them to me. He'll just call you up and say, hey, thanks for the sample, but we're hiring through Clayton. I think with these larger publishers, the ones I've named in Lombardi would be one of those too probably, you may have a little trouble getting in the front door with no experience. That isn't to say you shouldn't approach them though. I certainly would and show your samples to them. There are thousands of small 1,000, 2,000, 5,000 subscriber newsletters out there that you'll find in Oxbridge. Those are the ones that I would make sure that I have a healthy helping of those on any prospect list that I develop. I'm assuming you're using the formula that I laid out where you contact these people by a series of mailings. I just had this done to me, by the way, and it was wonderful. Last Wednesday in my new offices here, there was a 6x9 orange postcard. The postcard said, in two days, the most important package you have received all year will arrive in your mailbox. The guys from Agora were here visiting. I brought it up and I was waving it over my head. I said, look, one of the total package people has taken my advice. Lisa, if you're on the call, hi. I just can't wait to see what comes next. Sure enough, two days later, I get a DHL package. It's got this wonderful thing in there about how Harley's and marketing go together. She's a biker from Connecticut. It was just a really good example of great writing. This still wasn't the big package. The big package was not going to arrive for another couple of days. She had me sitting on pins and needles to see what she was going to do next. I basically had my own thing pulled on me. My own strategy pulled on me and I loved every minute of it. I called her yesterday and hired her. I was kind of laughing about it because it took grit. It took courage. By golly, she pulled it off. It worked. It was really well executed. I think the real opportunity here for beginning writers is with these smaller newsletters that are published by their editors rather than by big publishing companies. Do one or two of those. Produce some degree of success with those. Then you can walk into a Gora or Phillips or Healthy Directions or any of these other larger companies. You could do it in as little as 60, 90, 120 days with an actual sample under your eye. One other thing too that as you're talking about this that I'm thinking about as far as S or D S and Oxford both confirmed is there is a whole B2B aspect to the new butter industry as well. I was just going to say, let me speak to that. I want to add to what Clayton said. If you're going to want to start in the newsletter thing, you're going to, the newsletter industry. You're going to have difficulty writing off the bat for Phillips and the Gora and the big people. That's number one. However, there's a way to get inside them. One is to not necessarily start with doing a mag-a-log or a package for an acquisition, but they might want you to do a smaller piece of coffee. You might suggest that you do like a renewal or an advance or blanket renewal mailing. That's a huge demand Bob for web copy too. I was going to say web copy. You get it in that way. Short little emails, that kind of thing. Email blasts. You can do that even with the big people. Second market that Clayton talked about, which is people who publish consumer newsletters, but they're not the majors. There's some companies out there that have very good newsletters. Some of them have 3,000 to 5,000 subscribers. Some of them have 10,000, but they only publish 3 or 4 newsletters. They're not as bombarded by copywriters. They might be more open to you. The third, as Julie just said, is the business to business market. Here, you can go to the big publishers. There's a whole area of selling business newsletters to executives and small businesses. These newsletters typically charge $49 a year to $500 a year, depending on how specialized their topic is. There's very good money that can be made in doing that. These people, there's much less competition. Since we know that a lot of people that are coming into copywriting tend to be in coming in as a second leg in their career, they came from very specialized backgrounds where you could probably also find a lot of publications that speak specifically to what you did perhaps in your first career. You might actually be able to segue into writing for, let's say, if you were in printing or paper buying in your previous lifetime, there's a newsletter on paper buying. Julie, that's such a great idea because when you contact them, you say, if it's a newsletter that goes in the printing industry, and you say, I know the issues because I was a paper buyer for XYZ, or I was a press-pre-press operator or whatever, they're much more interested. The business to business newsletters are very interested in your specific knowledge and experience of their industry, and that's a good way to get into there. A couple more questions, and then we'll wrap because we're 115. I think we discussed this, but someone wants a clarification. The question is, what exactly does the term copy chief mean? Okay. A copy chief is basically the boss. It's used as a verb and a noun. Copy chiefing is another writer who is essentially reading and directing you. So the copy chief would typically work with you to develop the theme of the package and the major points that need to be covered in the package. And then would review each draft and would mark them up with comments, preferably with comments, not with actual changes in copy insertions, but saying, you're weak here. I don't believe this. Bad word choice. I'm getting bored. I deserve combat pay for this, for reading this. A copy chief is going to push you to produce the very best copy you can possibly produce. And the act of doing that is called copy chiefing. A mentor is someone who takes you by the hand and has a longer term relationship with you and moves you along. A copy chief can just be on one assignment. It's the person who's driving the copy process and who gets to say stick a fork in it. It's done. This is great. And gets to give you all of the negative reinforcement you need to get up to that point. So that's what copy chief is. We have time for, I think, one more question. And this is from Mark. He says, I think we've answered this, but people are still asking. You're right for a high-paying client and you blow it. The package bombs. Is that necessarily the end of your relationship with them or will they give you a second chance? Somebody wasn't on one of our calls. We kind of answered this. Yeah, we did. There are a lot of things that go into the successor failure of the package. And we went over it. To go back and listen to the calls, Clayton went over five or six factors that determined that. Actually, the copy could be great. It could be brilliant copy. It could be fantastic and just miss the market. Or in other words, it doesn't resonate with readers. It's a theme that everyone thought would, but it doesn't. But the very next, if the experience of working with you was a positive one, and if the copy itself was good, solid, professional, well-conceived, and executed copy, the publisher is not probably going to walk away from you. It would take a string of failures, of consistent failures before you would have to worry about that. Timing in the market. There are a lot of other factors. The graphic design might have been weak. List selection at smaller companies where they don't have a good list history could have been a problem. There could be any number of things contributing to the lack of success of a package. So don't panic. Everyone has failures. I have failures. Even now, after 30 some odd years, I have packages I really believe in that just didn't perform. Or packages where you write for a specific topical event and it doesn't happen. Yeah, we talked to that call about timing. I don't remember which call it was, but I think it might have been three. But yeah, we've done it where we talk about, for example, rising energy prices, and they're going to explode, and they're going to go to $100 a barrel for oil and everything. And the promotion just happens to drop. On the day the oil goes down? Yeah, on the day that oil starts a 30% decline that extends over about two or three months. You're going to get your head handed to you in a situation like that. So any number of reasons. And clients who've worked with, anybody that's worked in marketing for a period of time understands this, and they don't shoot the copywriter. The key is though keep plugging and watch out for those kinds of things and avoid themes that could reverse on you or things like that. And do your homework when it comes to the dominant emotions of your marketplace. And really feel like you can insert yourself in your prospect shoes so you won't have a misfire in terms of the package resonating with the prospect. On that note, I want to thank Julie. You guys can imagine it's a tremendous amount of work. We love having moved our business here onto the Internet, but it's the technical things that frustrate all of us. You buy these products and systems that say you can do this and that, and they don't work as easily as you would think. Those of you listening to Cole who have Internet marketing businesses, you know this. And Julie has done a tremendous amount of work. So I want to thank Julie, and of course most of all, I want to thank Clayton, who again, as we explained to Peter why he's doing this, we're lucky he is doing this. And as shared, I think, again, I've been in copywriting for 25 years, information and advice that has never been available. And these ideas, I do believe both in terms of copywriting as well as marketing yourself as a copywriter can help you take either your direct response business, if you have one, or a copywriting business to the next level. So I want to thank both of you guys. And I want to thank everybody that's been on these calls. You guys are great. I deeply appreciate your continued reading of the Total Package. Please, if these calls have been helpful to you at all, please drop us a note to feedback and make these Total Package.com. It'll help us encourage more people to join us in these calls and to help more people break into this great business and help more of our clients grow their companies faster. So let us hear from you. And I want to thank you again from the depth of my heart for joining us on these calls. Death of my heart for joining us on these calls.
